skill.name: metabolomics-workbench-database
skill.description: Use the NIH Metabolomics Workbench REST API to find studies, metabolites, RefMet names, m/z matches, structures, and gene or protein records.
skill.summary: Query public Metabolomics Workbench research data and metabolite reference records.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: AI review found the static critical and high findings to be false positives caused by markdown examples, public API URLs, and metabolomics notation. The skill is documentation-only and shows intended requests to the public NIH Metabolomics Workbench API; the residual risk is low and limited to user-initiated network access and one example file save.

low_findings.title: Public API Network Access Is Intended
low_findings.description: Static network findings are true indicators of outbound API examples, but they target the public Metabolomics Workbench REST service. The reviewed files are markdown documentation, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples for www.metabolomicsworkbench.org and no hidden endpoint or credential payload is present. Confidence is high because the files are documentation-only markdown.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misidentified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings correspond to markdown fenced examples and curl snippets. They do not execute when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are markdown delimiters around examples, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. No script file or executable command runner is present.

low_findings.title: Chemistry Notation Misidentified As Sensitive Or Crypto Pattern
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and weak cryptography findings are false positives from scientific terms and mass spectrometry adduct notation. No Windows credential database, password hash, or cryptographic implementation appears in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe MetStat URL format, ion adducts such as M+H and M-H, and output formats. There is no semantic evidence of credential access or weak cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: Documented File Save Example Requires User Control
low_findings.description: The only filesystem finding is a Python documentation example that writes a downloaded structure image. This is not executed by the skill, but users should choose safe output paths when adapting the example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write target is a fixed example filename in a markdown code sample. It is low risk because no runtime component performs the write automatically.

content.value_statement: Metabolomics researchers often need reliable study, compound, and m/z data from one public source. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through the Metabolomics Workbench REST API.
actual_capabilities: Find compound records by PubChem CID, InChI Key, KEGG ID, HMDB ID, formula, or registry number.
Retrieve study summaries, factors, metabolites, analysis details, data tables, and mwTab records.
Standardize metabolite names through RefMet and inspect classification hierarchy.
Search mass spectrometry candidates by m/z value, database, adduct, and tolerance.
Filter studies by platform, polarity, chromatography, species, sample source, disease, and metabolite.
Look up gene and protein records linked to metabolomics reference data.
limitations: It depends on the availability and response format of the public Metabolomics Workbench API.
It does not perform statistical testing or normalize raw metabolomics datasets.
It does not verify biological conclusions without user-provided study context.
Large result sets may require caching, batching, or manual review.

use_cases.title: Find studies for a metabolite
use_cases.description: Search public studies that measure a metabolite, then compare study summaries and experimental factors.

use_cases.title: Identify candidate compounds
use_cases.description: Use m/z values, adducts, and tolerances to find possible metabolite matches across supported databases.

use_cases.title: Standardize metabolite names
use_cases.description: Map common metabolite names to RefMet names and capture consistent classification metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Find a compound record
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Metabolomics Workbench to find the compound record for [identifier type] [identifier]. Summarize the name, formula, exact mass, and cross-references.

prompt_templates.title: Search studies by metabolite
prompt_templates.prompt: Standardize [metabolite name] with RefMet, then find Metabolomics Workbench studies that include it. Return study IDs, titles, species, and sample sources.

prompt_templates.title: Match m/z candidates
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for candidate compounds with m/z [value], adduct [adduct], tolerance [tolerance], and database [MB, LIPIDS, or REFMET]. Rank likely matches and note uncertainty.

prompt_templates.title: Compare disease-specific datasets
prompt_templates.prompt: Find human [sample source] studies for [disease] using [platform] and [polarity]. Compare available metadata, measured metabolites, and data access options.

output_examples.output: The response should list matching study IDs, study titles, species, sample sources, and available data formats. It should also state whether Tyrosine was normalized through RefMet.

output_examples.input: Search m/z 180.06 with M+H and 0.5 Da tolerance.
output_examples.output: The response should summarize candidate metabolites, registry numbers, exact masses, and database source. It should explain that m/z matching is tentative without retention time or MS/MS evidence.

output_examples.input: Standardize citrate and show its classification.
output_examples.output: The response should provide the RefMet preferred name, formula when available, and the super class, main class, and sub class returned by the API.

best_practices: Standardize names with RefMet before searching studies or combining results.
Choose m/z tolerances that match the instrument resolution and ionization method.
Cache repeated reference lookups during large reviews to reduce duplicate API requests.
anti_patterns: Do not treat m/z matches as confirmed identities without supporting experimental evidence.
Do not compare studies without checking species, sample source, platform, and data format.
Do not run large request batches without delays and clear output handling.

faq.question: Does this skill require authentication?
faq.answer: No. It is written for public Metabolomics Workbench REST API endpoints.

faq.question: What is RefMet?
faq.answer: RefMet is a standardized metabolite naming system with structural classification fields.

faq.question: Can it download full study data?
faq.answer: It can guide requests for study data and mwTab records when the public API provides them.

faq.question: Can it identify unknown metabolites?
faq.answer: It can produce candidate matches from m/z searches, but final identification needs additional evidence.

faq.question: Which analytical platforms are covered?
faq.answer: The documentation includes LC-MS, GC-MS, NMR, MS, and related MetStat filters.

faq.question: Can it analyze private datasets?
faq.answer: No. It provides API guidance and context, but users must supply any private data and analysis method.
